title: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma
authors: Caron A. Jacobson, Dan L. Longo

items.title: Prognosis Variability in NHL vs. HL
items.content: The prognosis and natural history of Non-Hodgkin's lymphomas (NHLs) are more variable compared to Hodgkin's lymphoma (HL), where approximately 80–85% of patients can be cured with chemotherapy, with or without radiotherapy.
keyPoints: Approximately 80-85% of HL patients are curable with chemotherapy and/or radiotherapy.
NHL prognosis is more variable than HL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma

items.title: Classification of NHL Based on Cell Type
items.content: Non-Hodgkin's lymphomas (NHLs) are classified based on the type of mature lymphocyte involved: B, T, or natural killer (NK) cells. This classification helps in understanding their biological and clinical characteristics.
keyPoints: NHLs can be categorized as either mature B-NHL or mature T/NK-NHL.
Classification is based on whether the cancerous lymphocyte is a B, T, or NK cell.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma

items.title: Incidence and Demographics of NHL
items.content: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) is a significant cancer burden, with an estimated 80,550 new cases in the U.S. annually, representing ~4% of all cancers. It ranks as the seventh most common cause of cancer-related death for both genders. The incidence is higher among Caucasians compared to African Americans and increases with age, particularly after 40.
keyPoints: 80,550 new cases estimated in the U.S. annually
~4% of all cancers
Seventh most common cause of cancer-related death
Higher incidence in Caucasians than African Americans
Incidence rises with age, especially after 40
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Geographic and Age-Related Variations in NHL Subtypes
items.content: The incidence of various NHL subtypes varies geographically and by age. T-cell lymphomas are more prevalent in Asia, while B-cell lymphomas like follicular lymphoma are common in Western countries. Aggressive lymphomas such as DLBCL and Burkitt's lymphoma predominate in children, whereas indolent forms like FL are more frequent in adults.
keyPoints: T-cell lymphomas more common in Asia
Follicular lymphoma prevalent in Western countries
DLBCL and Burkitt's lymphoma common in children
Indolent lymphomas like FL common in adults
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Infectious Agents Associated with NHL
items.content: Several infectious agents are linked to the development of specific NHL subtypes. Epstein-Barr virus is associated with Burkitt's lymphoma in Central Africa and aggressive NHL in immunosuppressed patients in Western countries. HTLV-1 leads to adult T-cell lymphoma, while Helicobacter pylori infection can cause gastric MALT lymphomas, which may regress with antibiotic treatment.
keyPoints: EBV linked to Burkitt's lymphoma and aggressive NHL
HTLV-1 causes adult T-cell lymphoma
Helicobacter pylori associated with gastric MALT lymphomas
Antibiotic treatment for H. pylori can regress MALT lymphoma
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Increased NHL Incidence in Immunosuppressed Patients
items.content: Patients undergoing organ transplantation, which necessitates chronic immunosuppression, have a nearly hundredfold increased incidence of Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma (NHL), particularly within the first year post-transplant. This association is significant because some lymphomas may regress if immunosuppression is reversed.
keyPoints: Incidence of NHL is nearly a hundredfold increased in patients undergoing organ transplantation.
Greatest incidence occurs in the first year post-transplant.
~30% of these cases arise from polyclonal B-cell proliferation evolving into clonal B-cell malignancy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Genetic and Familial Risk Factors in Lymphomas
items.content: An increased risk of NHL is observed among first-degree relatives with lymphoproliferative disorders such as NHL, Hodgkin Lymphoma (HL), or chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). Large database studies indicate that approximately 9% of patients with lymphoma or CLL have a first-degree relative affected by a similar disorder.
keyPoints: Increased risk of NHL in first-degree relatives with NHL, HL, or CLL.
~9% of patients with lymphoma or CLL have a first-degree relative with a lymphoproliferative disorder.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: B-cell Lymphomas: Genetic Events Leading to Oncogenesis
items.content: Most B-cell lymphomas arise from errors during immunoglobulin gene recombination and somatic hypermutation, which are normal processes in B-cell development. These genetic events can lead to chromosomal translocations that activate oncogenes or inactivate tumor-suppressor genes.
keyPoints: 90% of all lymphomas are of B-cell origin.
PAX5 is the master B lineage transcription factor involved in B-cell development.
Immunoglobulin gene recombination and somatic hypermutation can lead to oncogenesis due to their error-prone nature.
Common mutations involve MYC, BCL6, and BCL2 genes.

Examples of chromosomal translocations include (8;14)(q24;q32) involving MYC in Burkitt lymphoma (BL), (14;18)(q32;q32) involving BCL2 in follicular lymphoma (FL), and (11;14)(q13;q32) involving cyclin D1 in mantle cell lymphoma (MCL).
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: T-cell Lymphomas: Oncogenic Potential of TCR Gene Rearrangement
items.content: T-cell lymphomas can arise from errors during the rearrangement and recombination of T-cell receptor (TCR) genes, a process that is error-prone and potentially oncogenic.
keyPoints: T-cell differentiation requires NOTCH-1 expression upon migration to the thymus.
Development involves rearrangement and recombination of TCR loci.
Chromosomal translocations can involve T-cell antigen receptor genes on chromosomes 7 and 14.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: Diagnostic Value of Immunophenotyping in Lymphomas
items.content: The immunophenotype, or antigen footprint, of lymphoid cells is crucial for diagnosing specific non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL) subtypes. It can be detected using flow cytometry or immunohistochemical staining.
keyPoints: Immunophenotyping distinguishes specific NHL subtypes.
Flow cytometry and immunohistochemical staining are used for detection.
Cell-surface phenotype of lymphoid cells at stages of differentiation is not clinically or prognostically significant.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: Gene Profiling in Lymphoma: Diagnostic and Prognostic Significance
items.content: Gene profiling using array technology can identify new genes with pathologic importance, gene expression patterns with diagnostic/prognostic significance, and potential therapeutic targets. This has led to the identification of DLBCL subtypes with different prognoses based on GCB-like or ABC-like gene expression patterns.
keyPoints: Gene profiling allows assessment of thousands of genes simultaneously.
Identification of new lymphoma subtypes such as DLBCL with distinct gene expression patterns (GCB-like vs. ABC-like).
Patients with a GCB-like pattern have a better prognosis, independent of other prognostic factors.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.title: Importance of History and Physical Examination in Lymphoma Evaluation
items.content: A thorough history and physical examination are crucial for confirming lymphoma diagnosis, identifying urgent disease manifestations, aiding further study selection, and establishing patient rapport. They help distinguish between aggressive and indolent lymphomas based on symptom duration, progression pace, and presence of B symptoms.
keyPoints: Careful history and physical exam are essential in initial lymphoma evaluation.
Duration of symptoms and pace of symptomatic progression differentiate aggressive from indolent lymphomas.
Presence or absence of 'B' symptoms (fevers, night sweats, unexplained weight loss) is significant.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.title: Laboratory and Staging Studies in Lymphoma
items.content: Initial laboratory studies should include complete blood count, routine chemistries, liver function tests, serum protein electrophoresis, $  eta_{2} $-microglobulin level, and LDH. These are important for prognosis. Bone marrow biopsy may be needed for staging certain diseases, and lumbar puncture is indicated for CNS involvement risk or concerning neurologic signs.
keyPoints: Laboratory studies: CBC, routine chemistries, liver function tests, serum protein electrophoresis, $  eta_{2} $-microglobulin level, LDH.
$  eta_{2} $-microglobulin and LDH are independent prognostic factors in NHL.
Bone marrow biopsy may be required for staging; lumbar puncture indicated for CNS involvement risk.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.title: Imaging Studies for Lymphoma Staging
items.content: The choice of imaging studies in lymphoma is guided by the histology and clinical presentation. CT scans are essential for staging indolent lymphomas to assess lymphadenopathy, while FDG-PET is preferred for aggressive lymphomas due to its high sensitivity in detecting involved nodal and extranodal sites.
keyPoints: CT scans are essential for accurate staging of indolent lymphomas.
FDG-PET is useful for aggressive lymphomas like BL, DLBCL, plasmablastic lymphoma, and aggressive T-cell NHL.
PET scanning should be used for DLBCL and HL as per consensus recommendations from the International Harmonization Project.
End-of-treatment PET scan timing: not before 3 weeks, preferably 6–8 weeks after chemotherapy, and 8–12 weeks after radiation or chemoradiotherapy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.title: Prognostic Indices in Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
items.content: The International Prognostic Index (IPI) and Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index (FLIPI) are key tools for predicting outcomes in NHL. The IPI considers age, serum LDH, performance status, stage, and extranodal involvement to stratify patients into risk groups with corresponding survival rates.
keyPoints: IPI is based on analysis of >2000 patients with aggressive NHLs treated with an anthracycline-containing regimen.
Risk factors for IPI: age (≤60 vs >60), serum LDH (≤ normal vs > normal), performance status (0 or 1 vs 2-4), stage (I or II vs III or IV), extranodal involvement (<1 site vs >1 site).
FLIPI considers age >60, stage III/IV disease, >4 nodal sites, elevated serum LDH, and hemoglobin <12.
Median 10-year survival rates for FLIPI: 71% (low risk), 51% (intermediate risk), 36% (high risk).
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.title: Staging Systems and Treatment Implications in Lymphoma
items.content: The Ann Arbor staging system, while initially developed for Hodgkin lymphoma, is adapted for NHL but has limitations due to the widespread dissemination of NHL. Histology and clinical parameters are more critical than stage for prognosis, influencing treatment decisions.
keyPoints: Ann Arbor staging focuses on tumor sites, location, and systemic symptoms.
NHL often presents with advanced-stage disease at diagnosis.
Histology and clinical presentation are more important than stage for prognosis in NHL.
All stages of NHL except early-stage limited to a radiation field are treated similarly.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.title: Gene Expression Profiling and Immunohistochemical Algorithm for DLBCL
items.content: Gene expression profiling has identified two subtypes of Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL): Germinal Center B-cell-like (GCB) and Activated B-cell-like (ABC). GCB-like DLBCL is associated with a significantly better overall survival (OS). An immunohistochemical algorithm using CD10, BCL6, and MUM1 can differentiate between these subtypes. While these profiles have prognostic importance, they currently do not change primary treatment recommendations for DLBCL.
keyPoints: Gene expression profiling identifies GCB and ABC subtypes of DLBCL.
GCB-like DLBCL is associated with significantly better OS.
Immunohistochemical algorithm uses CD10, BCL6, and MUM1 to differentiate subtypes.
Prognostic importance but no current change in primary treatment recommendations.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 3)

items.title: Future Treatment Differentiation by DLBCL Subtype
items.content: Current clinical trials stratify DLBCL subtypes, indicating that agents like the Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor ibrutinib and lenalidomide are most effective in non-GCB DLBCL in relapsed cases. This suggests potential future differentiation of treatment based on these subtypes.
keyPoints: Clinical trials stratify by DLBCL subtype.
Ibrutinib and lenalidomide show activity in non-GCB DLBCL, especially in the relapsed setting.
Future treatments may be differentiated by DLBCL subtypes.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 3)

items.title: Burkitt's Lymphoma: Rapid Growth and Treatment Urgency
items.content: Burkitt's lymphoma is characterized by its rapid doubling time of less than 24 hours, necessitating immediate treatment. It accounts for less than 1% of NHL in adults but about 30% in childhood NHL. The disease presents in three major forms: endemic (African), nonendemic with abdominal presentation, and immunodeficiency-related cases. Treatment involves intensive chemotherapy regimens like Modified Magrath or dose-adjusted EPOCH-R, with CNS prophylaxis being mandatory.
keyPoints: Burkitt's lymphoma has a doubling time of <24 h.
It makes up <1% of NHL in adults and ~30% of childhood NHL.
Endemic BLs are EBV positive; nonendemic BLs are mostly EBV negative.
MYC translocation involving chromosome 8q24 occurs in >95% of cases.
Treatment must be initiated quickly due to rapid growth; cure rates are 80–90% with prompt treatment.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: Heterogeneity and Risk Factors
items.content: DLBCL is the most common histologic subtype of NHL, representing about one-third of cases. It is now recognized as a heterogeneous group rather than a single entity. The median age at diagnosis is 64, with a higher incidence in Caucasians and men. Patients with immunodeficiency or autoimmune disorders have an increased risk, often associated with EBV. Most patients present with advanced-stage disease, and up to 40% have extranodal involvement.
keyPoints: DLBCL represents about one-third of all NHL cases.
Median age at diagnosis is 64; higher incidence in Caucasians and men.
Relative risk is 3.5-fold among those with affected first-degree relatives.
~40% present with 'B' symptoms, and 50% have elevated LDH.
Up to 40% involve non-lymph node sites like bone marrow or CNS.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Aggressive vs. Indolent B-cell NHLs
items.content: B-cell NHLs are divided into aggressive and indolent categories. Aggressive types like DLBCL require immediate treatment with combination chemotherapy, while indolent types such as FL can be observed until symptomatic. MCL is intermediate-grade, needing urgent treatment but not curable with conventional doses.
keyPoints: Aggressive B-cell NHLs need immediate combination chemotherapy and are potentially curable.
Indolent B-cell NHLs like FL are treated when symptomatic; not ultimately curable.
MCL requires urgent treatment similar to aggressive lymphomas but shares indolent characteristics.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Prognosis of Double-Hit Lymphoma
items.content: Double-hit lymphomas, a subtype of high-grade B-cell lymphoma, are characterized by concurrent rearrangements in MYC and BCL2 genes. These lymphomas have an extremely poor prognosis with a median overall survival (OS) of only 12–18 months.
keyPoints: MYC is rearranged in 10% of DLBCLs
~20% of MYC-rearranged cases have concurrent BCL2 rearrangement
Double-hit lymphomas are associated with poor prognosis, median OS of 12-18 months
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Standard First-Line Chemotherapy for DLBCL
items.content: The addition of the anti-CD20 antibody rituximab to CHOP chemotherapy (R-CHOP) has been the standard first-line treatment for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), significantly improving survival compared to CHOP alone.
keyPoints: Rituximab added to CHOP improves survival
Standard first-line therapy: R-CHOP
~65–70% of patients with DLBCL can be cured with this approach
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Options for Relapsed or Refractory DLBCL
items.content: For patients with relapsed or refractory DLBCL, options include salvage chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation, CD19 CAR T cells, and newer agents like polatuzumab in combination therapies. Palliative treatments may involve less intensive chemotherapy or radiation.
keyPoints: Salvage chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation for relapses >12 months
CD19 CAR T cells for primary refractory or early relapsing disease
Newer agents: polatuzumab, tafasitamab, loncastuximab, epcoritamab, glofitamab
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.title: CAR-T Therapy for Relapsed/Refractory Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma
items.content: CD19 CAR-T therapies, such as axicabtagene ciloleucel and lisocabtagene autoleucel, are highly effective in treating patients with primary refractory or early relapsing disease. These therapies involve genetically modifying a patient's T cells to target CD19 on B-cell malignancies.
keyPoints: CD19 CAR-T therapies (axi-cel and liso-cel) are more effective than salvage chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplantation for primary refractory or early relapsing disease.
Response rate of CAR-T therapy in late relapsing, chemorefractory patients is >80%, with >50% achieving a complete response.
>40% of patients remain in remission at long-term follow-up after CAR-T therapy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Large B-Cell Lymphomas
items.content: Rituximab combined with CHOP chemotherapy improves outcomes in intravascular large B-cell lymphoma, while T-cell/histiocyte-rich large B-cell lymphoma has similar outcomes to DLBCL. R-CHOP is the treatment of choice for these lymphomas.
keyPoints: Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma prognosis improved with rituximab plus CHOP chemotherapy.
T-cell/histiocyte-rich large B-cell lymphoma outcomes are similar to those of DLBCL.
R-CHOP is the preferred treatment for these large B-cell lymphomas.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.title: Follicular Lymphoma Diagnosis and Grading
items.content: Follicular lymphoma diagnosis is based on morphologic findings, confirmed by immunophenotype and genetic markers like t(14;18) and BCL2 overexpression. It's graded from I to III based on the number of centroblasts per high-power field.
keyPoints: Follicular lymphoma accounts for 22% of NHLs worldwide, with a higher prevalence in the U.S.
Diagnosis involves confirming B-cell immunophenotype and genetic markers such as t(14;18) and BCL2 overexpression.
Grading based on centroblasts per high-power field: Grade I (0-5), II (6-15), III (>15).
Grade III is subdivided into IIIa (centrocytes predominate) and FLBCL (sheets of centroblasts).
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.title: Management and Treatment Strategies for Follicular Lymphoma
items.content: Follicular lymphoma (FL) often presents with painless lymphadenopathy, potentially involving multiple sites. While FL is sensitive to chemotherapy and radiotherapy, these are not curative except in early-stage disease. For early-stage FL, involved field radiotherapy can be curative, while observation is a viable alternative for asymptomatic patients.
keyPoints: FL presents with painless lymphadenopathy at multiple sites; extranodal presentations possible.
Histologic transformation to DLBCL occurs at ~3% per year.
Prognosis best predicted by FLIPI, staging typically done with CT scans of chest, abdomen, pelvis, and neck if suspected.

Involved field radiotherapy (24–30 Gy) can be curative in early-stage disease: 5-, 10-, and 15-year freedom from treatment failure rates are 72%, 46%, and 39% respectively; overall survival rates are 93%, 75%, and 62%.
Observation is a reasonable alternative for asymptomatic patients, with median time to treatment not reached at 7 years of follow-up.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.title: Indications and Outcomes for Systemic Treatment in Follicular Lymphoma
items.content: Systemic treatment options for FL include rituximab alone or combined with chemotherapy or lenalidomide. The choice of therapy depends on the indication and volume of disease. Rituximab monotherapy is effective for low-volume disease, while combination therapies like BR are preferred for medium- to high-volume FL.
keyPoints: Rituximab alone has a ~70% response rate with median duration >2 years; maintenance rituximab improves response duration.
Combination of rituximab with chemotherapy (e.g., CHOP, CVP) improves survival for larger volume disease.
Bendamustine plus rituximab (BR) is standard care for medium- to high-volume FL due to longer response duration and less toxicity compared to R-CHOP.
Obinutuzumab with chemotherapy shows improved MRD negativity and progression-free survival but more infections and infusion reactions compared to rituximab plus chemotherapy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.title: Comparative Efficacy of Treatment Regimens for Follicular Lymphoma
items.content: Several treatment regimens are available for FL, with no established superiority between rituximab plus chemotherapy and obinutuzumab plus chemotherapy. Rituximab combined with lenalidomide offers a chemotherapy-free option with similar outcomes to traditional chemotherapy combinations.
keyPoints: Both rituximab plus chemotherapy and obinutuzumab plus chemotherapy are viable options for untreated FL.
No established superiority between rituximab plus chemotherapy and obinutuzumab plus chemotherapy.
Rituximab plus lenalidomide is a chemotherapy-free regimen with similar outcomes to BR, R-CHOP, or R-CVP.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Relapsed Follicular Lymphoma
items.content: In relapsed follicular lymphoma (FL), treatment options include rituximab, obinutuzumab, targeted therapies like lenalidomide and tazemetostat, and CAR-T therapies such as axi-cel and tisa-cel. Autologous and allogeneic stem cell transplants are considered for multiply relapsed FL.
keyPoints: Single-agent rituximab or alternative chemotherapy regimens with rituximab and obinutuzumab can be used in relapsed FL.
Autologous and allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplants yield high complete response rates: 40% and 60%, respectively, but are associated with significant morbidity and mortality.
>80% of patients with multiply relapsed FL show complete responses to CAR-T therapies like axi-cel and tisa-cel.
Mosunetuzumab offers a potentially less toxic option compared to CAR-T therapy for third-line or later treatment.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.title: Histologic Transformation in Follicular Lymphoma
items.content: Follicular lymphoma has a high rate of transformation to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), occurring at approximately 3% per year. This transformation is often indicated by rapid growth and systemic symptoms.
keyPoints: Approximately 3% per year, FL transforms to DLBCL.
Transformation is recognized ~40% of the time during illness by repeat biopsy.
R-CHOP chemotherapy can be curative for untreated FL transforming to DLBCL.
Prognosis is poor for previously treated FL that transforms; aggressive therapy and autologous stem cell transplant are recommended.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.title: Management of Nodal Marginal Zone Lymphoma
items.content: Nodal marginal zone lymphoma (MZL) is managed similarly to FL, with the addition of Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors like ibrutinib and zanubrutinib being highly effective in relapsed cases.
keyPoints: Nodal MZL resembles FL clinically and is treated similarly.
BTK inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are active in nodal MZL, unlike in FL.
CAR-T therapy has shown similar efficacy to FL in clinical trials for MZL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.title: Splenic MZL: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Splenic Marginal Zone Lymphoma (MZL) is primarily seen in older Caucasian patients, with hepatitis C infection being a risk factor. The lymphoma can regress with treatment of the virus. Diagnosis involves flow cytometry showing malignant lymphocytes positive for CD19, CD20, and surface immunoglobulin but negative for CD5 and CD10. Splenectomy offers significant symptom relief and is effective in 85% of patients.
keyPoints: Risk factor: Hepatitis C infection
Diagnostic markers: Positive for CD19, CD20; Negative for CD5, CD10
Splenectomy response rate: 85%
Progression-free survival at 5 years: 58%
Overall survival at 5 years: 77%
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.title: MALT Lymphoma: Pathogenesis and Management
items.content: Mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) lymphoma is linked to chronic inflammation from autoimmune diseases or infections. It often presents with stage I or II disease, treatable by radiation or antibiotics targeting the underlying infection. Recurrent cytogenetic abnormalities include t(11;18), which activates NF-κB.
keyPoints: Common sites: Stomach, skin, salivary glands
Associated conditions: Chronic infections (e.g., H. pylori)
Cytogenetic abnormality: t(11;18) in up to 50% of cases
Treatment for stage I/II: Radiation or antibiotics
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.title: Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphoma: Characteristics and Treatment Options
items.content: Lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma (LPL) is an indolent B-cell NHL, often presenting with Waldenström's macroglobulinemia due to IgM paraprotein. Over 90% have MYD88 mutations. Treatment includes rituximab and chemoimmunotherapy; plasmapheresis can relieve hyperviscosity symptoms.
keyPoints: Association: Waldenström's macroglobulinemia
MYD88 mutation prevalence: >90%
Symptoms: Hyperviscosity from IgM paraprotein
Treatment options: Rituximab, chemoimmunotherapy, plasmapheresis
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.title: Prognostic Factors in Mantle Cell Lymphoma
items.content: The prognosis of Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) is influenced by various factors including the MCL International Prognostic Index (MIPI), which considers age, performance status, LDH levels, white blood cell count, and Ki67 expression. High-risk features include mutations in TP53 or a complex karyotype.
keyPoints: MCL composes ~6% of Non-Hodgkin Lymphomas (NHLS).
Median survival is 5–10 years; high-risk variants have median OS of only 18 months.
Prognosis predicted by MIPI: factors include age, performance status, LDH, white blood cell count, and Ki67 expression.
>90% of MCL cases show t(11;14) translocation involving cyclin D1.
High-risk features: TP53 mutation or complex karyotype.
Over two-thirds present with stage IV disease at diagnosis.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 7)

items.title: Clinical Variants and Management of Mantle Cell Lymphoma
items.content: Mantle Cell Lymphoma has clinical variants that influence management strategies. The indolent SOX11 variant with low Ki67 (<10%) can be observed until symptoms or organ function impairment occur, while the aggressive blastic variant with high Ki67 is associated with poor prognosis.
keyPoints: Indolent SOX11 variant: presents with circulating disease and splenomegaly, low Ki67 (<10%).
Blastic variant: high Ki67 index, median OS of 18 months.
Aggressive treatment often required, including chemoimmunotherapy or autologous stem cell transplant.
Common extranodal involvement in the gastrointestinal tract with diffuse lymphomatous polyposis.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 7)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for MCL
items.content: Management of Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL) has evolved with various treatment strategies showing differing levels of efficacy. While R-CHOP is standard, it often fails to achieve complete remission in disseminated disease. High-dose cytarabine and autologous stem cell transplantation improve progression-free survival but not cure rates. BR-based regimens are more effective than R-CHOP, with combinations including cytarabine or BTK inhibitors like ibrutinib showing promise. Maintenance rituximab post-induction improves outcomes, and high-dose chemotherapy may no longer be beneficial upfront.
keyPoints: R-CHOP is unsatisfactory for disseminated MCL, with few achieving complete remission.
High-dose cytarabine plus R-CHOP-like regimens improve progression-free survival but not cure rates.
BR-based therapies are more effective than R-CHOP.
Combining BR with cytarabine or BTK inhibitors like ibrutinib shows efficacy and tolerability.
Maintenance rituximab post-induction improves outcomes over observation alone.
High-dose chemotherapy may not benefit patients upfront with modern induction therapies.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 8)

items.title: Relapsed MCL Treatment Options
items.content: For relapsed MCL, BTK inhibitors such as ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, and zanubrutinib show a response rate of almost 70% but with limited duration (18–24 months). These are being explored in combination therapies. Anti-CD19-directed CAR-T therapy achieves complete responses in two-thirds of patients post-chemoimmunotherapy and BTK inhibition, potentially offering curative outcomes. Other agents like lenalidomide, venetoclax, bortezomib, and temsirolimus induce transient partial responses. Allogeneic stem cell transplantation is recommended for appropriate responders to salvage therapy (excluding CAR-T) and can lead to long-term disease-free survival in 30–50% of patients.
keyPoints: BTK inhibitors have a response rate of ~70% but last only 18-24 months.
CAR-T therapies achieve complete responses in two-thirds of relapsed/refractory MCL cases.
Potential curative outcomes with CAR-T therapy, pending longer follow-up.
Allogeneic stem cell transplant leads to long-term disease-free survival in 30–50% of patients post-salvage therapy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 8)

items.title: Understanding Mycosis Fungoides: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Mycosis fungoides, a type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, often presents with indolent skin lesions that progress from patches to plaques to tumors. Early diagnosis can be challenging due to non-specific biopsy results, requiring longitudinal observation for confirmation.
keyPoints: Median age of onset is in the mid-fifties; more common in males and blacks.
Progression: patch stage → plaque stage → cutaneous tumors.
Localized early-stage mycosis fungoides can be cured with radiotherapy, such as total-skin electron beam irradiation.
Advanced disease treatments include topical therapies, phototherapy, systemic cytotoxic therapy, and mogamulizumab (FDA approved).
Treatments are generally palliative.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of PTCL NOS: Diagnosis and Prognosis
items.content: Peripheral T-cell Lymphoma, Not Otherwise Specified (PTCL NOS), accounts for a significant portion of adult NHLs. It presents in older individuals with advanced-stage disease at diagnosis, often involving multiple organs.
keyPoints: Constitutes 6% of all NHLs; PTCLs make up 15% of adult NHLs.
Median age at presentation is 65 years.
Commonly involves bone marrow, liver, spleen, and skin with associated 'B' symptoms and pruritis.
IPI applied to PTCL NOS shows even low-risk group has a median OS of just >2 years.
Histology varies; often positive for CD3, majority for CD4 over CD8.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.title: Primary Therapy Approaches for PTCL NOS
items.content: The primary treatment strategy for PTCL NOS involves CHOP-like chemotherapy regimens, with variations depending on patient age and disease risk factors.
keyPoints: Common therapy: CHOP or CHOEP (CHOP + etoposide).
CHOEP may benefit younger patients and those with favorable risk factors.
Brentuximab combined with cyclophosphamide is another therapeutic option.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.title: Brentuximab Vedotin Improves Outcomes in CD30+ T-cell Lymphomas
items.content: In randomized clinical trials, the addition of brentuximab vedotin to CHOP chemotherapy has shown improved progression-free survival compared to CHOP alone for patients with CD30+ T-cell lymphomas, particularly ALCL.
keyPoints: Brentuximab-containing regimen (CHP) shows better progression-free survival than CHOP in CD30+ T-cell lymphomas.
Most pronounced benefit is observed in patients with Anaplastic Large-Cell Lymphoma (ALCL).
Approved frontline treatment for CD30+ T-cell lymphomas, including ALCL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.title: Characteristics and Prognosis of Anaplastic Large-Cell Lymphoma (ALCL)
items.content: ALCL is characterized by the presence of hallmark cells, CD30 positivity, and often involves ALK rearrangements. The prognosis varies significantly based on ALK status, with ALK-positive cases having a much better outcome.
keyPoints: ALK-positive ALCL has an 8-year overall survival rate of 82%.
ALK-negative ALCL has a lower 8-year OS rate of 49%.
Median age at diagnosis is 34 for ALK-positive and 58 for ALK-negative patients.
Tumor cells are CD30 positive, often express CD3, CD25, CD43, and CD4.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Relapsed ALCL
items.content: For relapsed ALK-positive ALCL, treatment approaches include salvage chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplant. Brentuximab vedotin is highly effective in cases resistant to chemotherapy or in ALK-negative disease.
keyPoints: Relapsed ALK-positive ALCL treated similarly to relapsed DLBCL with chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplant.
Brentuximab vedotin shows high activity (86% response rate, 57% complete response) in refractory or ALK-negative cases.
Conjugated anti-CD30 antibody brentuximab is a key treatment for relapsed disease.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.title: Enteropathy-associated T-cell Lymphoma and its Management
items.content: Enteropathy-type intestinal T-cell lymphoma is divided into two types: Type I, which occurs in patients with gluten-sensitive enteropathy and can be prevented by a gluten-free diet; and Type II (monomorphic epitheliotropic), not associated with celiac disease. The prognosis for this disorder is poor, with a median survival of 10 months.
keyPoints: Type I is associated with HLADQA1'0501, DQB1'0201
A gluten-free diet can prevent Type I development
Median survival is 10 months
Treatment often involves combination chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplant in first remission
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)

items.title: Hepatosplenic γδ T-cell Lymphoma Characteristics and Treatment
items.content: This systemic illness presents with sinusoidal infiltration of the liver, spleen, and bone marrow by malignant T cells. It is associated with genetic events like isochromosome 7q and trisomy 8. Despite poor outcomes generally, treatment regimens including ifosfamide (e.g., ICE or IVAC) show better results in small patient series.
keyPoints: Systemic symptoms often present
Associated genetic events: isochromosome 7q, trisomy 8
Treatment includes ifosfamide-based regimens like ICE or IVAC
Responding patients should consider allogeneic stem cell transplantation
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)

items.title: Adult T-cell Leukemia/Lymphoma: Variants and Prognosis
items.content: ATLL, driven by HTLV-1, has four variants with varying prognoses. The acute variant has a median survival of 6 months, lymphomatous 10 months, chronic 24 months, and smoldering not yet reached. Treatment includes combination chemotherapy, antiretrovirals like zidovudine, interferon α, arsenic, and mogamulizumab.
keyPoints: Most prevalent in Japan and the Caribbean
Disease variants: acute (60%), lymphomatous (20%), chronic (15%), smoldering (5%)
Median survival times: 6 months (acute), 10 months (lymphomatous), 24 months (chronic), not yet reached (smoldering)
Treatment includes combination chemotherapy, zidovudine, interferon α, arsenic, mogamulizumab
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)

items.statement: The incidence of NHL is decreased for patients undergoing organ transplantation.
items.explanation: The incidence of NHL is nearly a hundredfold increased for patients undergoing organ transplantation.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: Most B-cell lymphomas arise following the process of T-cell receptor gene rearrangement.
items.explanation: B-cell lymphomas arise following immunoglobulin gene recombination and somatic hypermutation, not T-cell receptor gene rearrangement.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.statement: The (8;14)(q24;q32) translocation in Burkitt lymphoma involves the MYC proto-oncogene and the IgH gene.
items.explanation: This specific chromosomal translocation is indeed involved in Burkitt lymphoma.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.statement: Gene profiling using array technology can identify new therapeutic targets in lymphomas.
items.explanation: This technology provides the possibility to identify new genes with pathologic importance and new therapeutic targets.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.statement: The presence of 'B' symptoms is not important in distinguishing between aggressive and indolent lymphomas.
items.explanation: The presence or absence of 'B' symptoms, such as fevers, night sweats, or unexplained weight loss, are important in distinguishing aggressive from more indolent lymphomas.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.statement: The Ann Arbor staging system is highly effective for NHL due to its stepwise dissemination pattern.
items.explanation: The Ann Arbor staging system is less useful in NHL, which disseminates widely and not in an ordered stepwise fashion.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.statement: PET scans are recommended for long-term follow-up of all lymphoma types.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that long-term follow-up should include PET scanning.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.statement: Gene expression profiling has altered treatment recommendations for primary treatment of DLBCL.
items.explanation: To date, gene expression profiles do not alter treatment recommendations for the primary treatment of DLBCL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 3)

items.statement: Current clinical trials stratify by DLBCL subtype.
items.explanation: Clinical trials do stratify by DLBCL subtype.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 3)

items.statement: Burkitt's lymphoma is more common in adults than in children in the United States.
items.explanation: Burkitt's lymphoma is rare in adults and makes up less than 1% of NHL in the U.S., but about 30% of childhood NHL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.statement: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma is considered a homogeneous disease.
items.explanation: DLBCL is now recognized as a heterogeneous collection of multiple entities.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.statement: Approximately 20% of MYC-rearranged DLBCL cases also have a concurrent BCL6 rearrangement.
items.explanation: Approximately 20% of MYC-rearranged cases have a concurrent BCL2 rearrangement, not BCL6.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.statement: The FLYER studies showed that extending R-CHOP chemotherapy from four to six cycles provided significant benefits for early favorable-risk DLBCL.
items.explanation: The FLYER studies showed no benefit to a more extended course of chemotherapy in early favorable-risk DLBCL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.statement: The response rate of CAR-T cells in late relapsing patients with chemorefractory disease is >80%.
items.explanation: CAR-T therapies have a response rate of >80% in late relapsing patients with chemorefractory disease.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.statement: Follicular Lymphoma is primarily diagnosed based on clinical symptoms.
items.explanation: Follicular Lymphoma can be accurately diagnosed on morphologic findings alone, not primarily based on clinical symptoms.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.statement: Treating patients with asymptomatic follicular lymphoma improves survival compared to observation.
items.explanation: Numerous studies have shown that treating asymptomatic disease does not improve survival compared with close observation.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.statement: PET/CT scans are useful in cases where transformation of follicular lymphoma is suspected.
items.explanation: PET/CT scans can be helpful when disease transformation is suspected, as transformed disease will be more FDG avid than indolent disease.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.statement: The PI3 kinase inhibitors idelalisib, duvelisib, and copanlisib are currently available for the treatment of FL.
items.explanation: These PI3 kinase inhibitors are no longer available due to lack of randomized confirmatory evidence supporting their efficacy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.statement: CAR-T therapies axi-cel and tisa-cel have been approved for the treatment of relapsed FL in third-line settings.
items.explanation: Axi-cel and tisa-cel are indeed approved for relapsed FL in third-line settings and beyond.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.statement: Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphoma is associated with a monoclonal IgG paraprotein.
items.explanation: Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphoma is most commonly associated with a monoclonal IgM paraprotein.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.statement: The t(11;18) translocation in MALT lymphomas results in the fusion of API2 and MALT1 genes.
items.explanation: The t(11;18) translocation leads to the fusion of the apoptosis inhibitor 2 (API2) gene and the MALT1 gene.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.statement: Over two-thirds of patients with Mantle Cell Lymphoma are diagnosed at stage IV.
items.explanation: Most patients have stage IV disease, primarily involving bone marrow and peripheral blood.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 7)

items.statement: The average age of diagnosis for Mantle Cell Lymphoma is below 50 years.
items.explanation: The average age of diagnosis for Mantle Cell Lymphoma is 63.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 7)

items.statement: High-dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell rescue are beneficial as frontline therapy for MCL with modern induction therapies.
items.explanation: Modern induction therapies suggest that high-dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell rescue may no longer provide benefit in the frontline setting.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 8)

items.statement: Anti-CD19-directed CAR-T therapy has achieved complete responses in two-thirds of patients with relapsed/refractory MCL.
items.explanation: CAR-T therapies have resulted in complete responses in about two-thirds of patients who progressed after other treatments.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 8)

items.statement: Mycosis fungoides is more often seen by internists than dermatologists.
items.explanation: Mycosis fungoides is more often seen by dermatologists due to its cutaneous manifestations.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.statement: PTCL NOS accounts for 15% of all non-Hodgkin lymphomas in adults.
items.explanation: PTCLs, including PTCL NOS, account for 15% of all NHLs in adults, but PTCL NOS alone composes 6%.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.statement: Most cases of Angioimmunoblastic T-Cell Lymphoma (AITL) present with early-stage disease at diagnosis.
items.explanation: Over 80% of AITL patients have advanced-stage disease at diagnosis.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.statement: The ALK inhibitors, including crizotinib, are active in refractory ALK-positive Anaplastic Large-Cell Lymphoma (ALCL).
items.explanation: ALK inhibitors like crizotinib show excellent outcomes in refractory ALK-positive ALCL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.statement: Type II enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma is associated with celiac disease.
items.explanation: Type II enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma, now called monomorphic epitheliotropic intestinal T-cell lymphoma, is not associated with celiac disease.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)

items.statement: Subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma tends to occur in young men in their fifth and sixth decades of life.
items.explanation: This disorder is indeed a disease of young men typically in their fifth and sixth decades.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)

items.assertion: The incidence of NHL has nearly doubled over the past 20–40 years.
items.reason: Patients with both primary and secondary immunodeficiency states are predisposed to developing NHL.
items.explanation: While it is true that the incidence of NHL has increased, the reason given does not explain this increase.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Infection with Helicobacter pylori leads to gastric MALT lymphomas.
items.reason: The bacterium transforms lymphocytes directly to produce the lymphoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because it's the immune response to the bacterium that causes neoplasia, not direct transformation.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Immune dysregulation contributes to the pathogenesis of some lymphomas.
items.reason: The elevated incidence of lymphoma in iatrogenic immunosuppression, AIDS, and autoimmune disease supports this.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND ETIOLOGY (part 2)

items.assertion: The error-prone nature of immunoglobulin gene recombination contributes to the development of B-cell lymphomas.
items.reason: Chromosomal translocations during this process can activate oncogenes or inactivate tumor-suppressor genes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why immunoglobulin gene recombination contributes to B-cell lymphomas.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.assertion: The stage of differentiation of a malignant lymphoma predicts its natural history.
items.reason: Lymphoid malignancies often retain the cell-surface phenotype of lymphoid cells at particular stages of differentiation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the stage of differentiation does not predict the natural history, though the reason is true.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · IMMUNOLOGY

items.assertion: Patients with DLBCL having a GCB-like gene expression pattern have a better prognosis.
items.reason: This improved prognosis is independent of other known prognostic factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with a GCB-like pattern have a better prognosis.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.assertion: A lumbar puncture is always required for staging in non-Hodgkin lymphoma.
items.reason: It may be indicated in the setting of concerning neurologic signs or symptoms or high-risk diseases for CNS involvement.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a lumbar puncture is not always required, but it may be indicated under certain conditions.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 1)

items.assertion: The International Prognostic Index (IPI) is a predictor of outcome in aggressive NHLs.
items.reason: It was developed based on the analysis of over 2000 patients with aggressive NHLs treated with an anthracycline-containing regimen.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why IPI is a predictor.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.assertion: The Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index (FLIPI) includes cell-surface markers in its predictive model.
items.reason: FLIPI is derived from the analysis of over 4000 patients and identifies independent prognostic variables for FL.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because FLIPI does not include cell-surface markers; it includes factors like age, stage, nodal sites, LDH concentration, and hemoglobin levels.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 2)

items.assertion: Gene expression profiling has identified DLBCLs with differential prognoses.
items.reason: GCB-like DLBCL is associated with a significantly better overall survival compared to non-GCB-like DLBCL.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · APPROACH TO THE PATIENT (part 3)

items.assertion: Burkitt's lymphoma requires rapid treatment initiation.
items.reason: It has an exquisitely chemosensitive nature and a doubling time of less than 24 hours.
items.explanation: The rapid doubling time and high morbidity necessitate quick treatment initiation for Burkitt's lymphoma.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.assertion: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma is more common in patients with autoimmune disorders.
items.reason: Patients on immunosuppression have a higher risk of developing DLBCL, often EBV-related.
items.explanation: While both statements are true, the reason does not specifically explain why DLBCL is more common in patients with autoimmune disorders.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 1)

items.assertion: Combination chemotherapy with R-CHOP is potentially curative for DLBCL.
items.reason: Rituximab, when added to CHOP, improves survival beyond CHOP alone.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why R-CHOP is potentially curative.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.assertion: CNS prophylaxis with intrathecal chemotherapy or high-dose systemic methotrexate offers clear benefits to patients at high risk for CNS relapse.
items.reason: Retrospective and observational studies show unclear benefit, leading to decreased use of CNS prophylaxis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the benefit is unclear; however, the reason correctly states why its use has decreased.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with Follicular Lymphoma are often subclassified into grades I to III based on the number of centroblasts per high-power field.
items.reason: Grade III has been subdivided into grade IIIa and follicular large B-cell lymphoma, which have prognostic significance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.assertion: The addition of rituximab to CHOP chemotherapy has not changed outcomes for intravascular large B-cell lymphoma.
items.reason: Rituximab combined with CHOP chemotherapy has dramatically improved outcomes in intravascular large B-cell lymphoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 3)

items.assertion: Rituximab alone can achieve a response rate of ~70% in patients with low-volume follicular lymphoma.
items.reason: Single-agent rituximab is associated with a response rate of ~70% and a median response duration of >2 years for low-volume disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.assertion: The superiority of rituximab plus chemotherapy over obinutuzumab plus chemotherapy has been established.
items.reason: Obinutuzumab combinations resulted in improvements in MRD negativity and progression-free survival at the expense of more infection and infusion reactions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the superiority of one regimen over the other has not been established, although both have shown benefits.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 4)

items.assertion: Patients with follicular lymphoma who do not achieve a complete metabolic response by PET/CT scanning to their primary therapy tend to have poorer outcomes with chemotherapy.
items.reason: These patients should be considered for non-chemotherapy options such as lenalidomide-based therapy or CAR-T therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of why these patients might have poorer outcomes with chemotherapy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.assertion: Grade IIIb follicular lymphoma should be treated as diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
items.reason: Grade IIIb FL is more similar to DLBCL than it is to typical follicular lymphoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why grade IIIb FL should be treated as DLBCL.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 5)

items.assertion: Splenectomy can be a curative treatment for stage I or II MALT lymphoma.
items.reason: Radiation therapy may be curative in cases of stage I or II MALT lymphoma.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion as splenectomy is not mentioned for curative treatment in MALT lymphoma.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.assertion: Activating mutations in MYD88 are present in less than 50% of Lymphoplasmacytic Lymphomas.
items.reason: MYD88 is an adaptor protein involved in signaling downstream of the Ig receptor leading to NF-κB activation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because activating mutations in MYD88 are present in >90% of cases, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE B-CELL NEOPLASMS (part 6)

items.assertion: PTCL NOS is a heterogeneous group of lymphomas.
items.reason: Histology, immunophenotype, and genetics are variable in PTCL NOS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the variability in histology, immunophenotype, and genetics explains the heterogeneity of PTCL NOS.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.assertion: The majority of PTCL NOS cases are positive for CD8.
items.reason: PTCL NOS often shows loss of mature T-cell markers like CD5 and CD7.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the majority of PTCL NOS is positive for CD4 rather than CD8.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with ALK-negative Anaplastic Large-Cell Lymphoma have a better prognosis than those with ALK-positive disease.
items.reason: ALK-negative patients have an 8-year OS rate of 82% compared to 49% for ALK-positive patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; ALK-positive patients actually have a better prognosis than ALK-negative ones.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.assertion: Autologous stem cell transplant can improve progression-free survival in certain contexts for mature peripheral T-cell disorders.
items.reason: This treatment is considered for patients achieving first remission.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with aggressive subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma should be managed with multiagent chemotherapy.
items.reason: Lymphomas that express gamma/delta TCRs are more aggressive and associated with a worse prognosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why aggressive subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma is managed with multiagent chemotherapy.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)

items.assertion: The prognosis for patients with acute adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma is better than those with the smoldering variant.
items.reason: Acute ATLL has a median survival of 6 months, while the smoldering variant's median survival time has not yet been reached.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because acute ATLL has a worse prognosis than the smoldering variant.
items.reference: Non Hodgkin s Lymphoma · MATURE (PERIPHERAL) T-CELL DISORDERS (part 3)
